food_name: Sisig (Filipino sizzling chopped pork face, ears, and liver with onions, calamansi, chilli, and egg)

food_subgroup: Sizzling minced pork offal dish (pig face, ears, liver) with onions, calamansi, chilli, and egg; served on cast iron plate

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Sisig. No sufficiently specific FoodOn term for Filipino sizzling pork offal dish as of 2026. Closest parent would be a generic pork offal dish term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork offal dish — closest parent match; no FoodEx2 code specific to Sisig exists
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0AAV used as closest parent for pork offal dish. No Sisig-specific FoodEx2 code exists in the current hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Sisig or Filipino pork offal dish identified in available SNOMED CT hierarchy.

preparation_method: topped with raw egg cooked on hot plate

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Sisig is one sizzling cast iron plate containing approximately 300g of chopped pork face, ears, and liver with onions, calamansi, chilli, and a whole egg on top.

nutrient_source.title: Sisig — FNRI Philippines Food Composition Table (PFCT) combined with USDA SR Legacy pork offal entries
nutrient_source.organization: FNRI-DOST Philippines (Philippine Food Composition Table) and USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived from FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT for Filipino pork offal dishes combined with USDA SR Legacy data for pork face, pork ears, and pork liver components. Values represent a composite of the traditional Kapampangan sisig recipe at approximately 300g per sizzling plate serving. USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) used as secondary cross-reference for individual pork offal nutrient values.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI based on macronutrient composition: predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (~2g per 100g; ~6g per 300g serving). Analogous to very-low-carbohydrate pork dishes in University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate) — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sisig in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~15 based on the dish's macronutrient profile: ~2g carbohydrate per 100g (6g per 300g serving), with the remainder being protein (~12.7g/100g) and fat (~13.3g/100g). This is consistent with very-low-carbohydrate pork and offal dishes. GL per 300g serving = (15 × 6) / 100 = 0.9. Estimation methodology consistent with Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed GI estimation framework.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (~900mg per 300g serving, ~45% of 2000mg daily limit) and very high cholesterol (~348mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit Sisig to occasional consumption (no more than twice monthly), request no additional soy sauce or fish sauce (patis) at the table, and be aware that restaurant versions may contain additional sodium from mayonnaise or seasoning; the high cholesterol content (~348mg per serving) also poses long-term cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~6g per 300g serving), GI ~15, GL ~0.9 — minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sisig is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management from a glycaemic perspective due to its very low carbohydrate content (~6g per serving) and negligible glycaemic load (~0.9); however, the high saturated fat (~14.4g per serving) and cholesterol (~348mg per serving) require monitoring for postprandial lipemia and long-term cardiovascular risk in diabetic patients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high cholesterol (~348mg per 300g serving, exceeding the 300mg daily recommended limit) and high saturated fat (~14.4g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Sisig only occasionally due to its very high cholesterol content (~348mg per 300g serving, which alone exceeds the 300mg daily limit) and high saturated fat (~14.4g per serving); the pig liver component contributes significant dietary cholesterol and should be factored into total daily lipid intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat promotes systemic inflammation; pig liver provides beneficial B12, folate, and iron for RA patients on methotrexate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sisig provides clinically relevant micronutrients for RA patients on methotrexate (B12 ~2.5mcg, folate ~105mcg DFE, iron ~6mg per serving), and the calamansi vitamin C enhances non-haem iron absorption; however, the high saturated fat (~14.4g per serving) promotes systemic inflammation and should be balanced against these benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sisig is a Filipino sizzling dish originating from Pampanga province (Kapampangan cuisine), created by Chef Lucia Cunanan ('Nanay Lucing') in Angeles City in the 1970s and recognised by TIME Magazine as one of the world's best street foods. It is made from finely chopped pig face, pig ears, and pig liver, mixed with onions, calamansi (Philippine lime), and chilli, then served on a cast iron sizzling plate with a whole egg on top that cooks from the residual heat. The texture is crispy-chewy from the grilled pig skin and ears, with a sour-savoury-spicy flavour profile from the calamansi and chilli. Per 300g sizzling plate serving: approximately 561 kcal, 38g protein, 40g fat (~14.4g saturated), 6g carbohydrate, GI ~15, GL ~0.9, ~900mg sodium, ~348mg cholesterol. The pig liver provides significant micronutrients: vitamin A (~360mcg RAE), B12 (~2.5mcg), folate (~105mcg DFE), and iron (~6mg) per serving. Sisig has an excellent glycaemic profile (GI ~15, GL ~0.9) making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes from a blood sugar perspective, but its very high cholesterol and saturated fat content make it a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease and hypertension. Available at Filipino restaurants in Singapore (Golden Mile Complex, Orchard Road). Source: FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT combined with USDA SR Legacy pork offal data.
meal_context: Filipino lunch or dinner main dish

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT combined with USDA SR Legacy pork offal component data. No single approved-source entry exists for the complete Sisig dish as prepared; values represent a defensible composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on macronutrient composition rather than a direct measurement. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite/adapted nature of the source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite Sisig dish (pig face, ears, liver, onions, egg) is not available as a single entry in FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026; individual pork offal fatty acid data exists but cannot be reliably combined without recipe-level proportional data. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite Sisig dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for pork offal and egg preparation without industrial hydrogenated fats). GI of ~15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sisig exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Sisig originates from Pampanga province, Philippines, and is available at Filipino restaurants in Singapore including Golden Mile Complex. The food_subgroup in the input was truncated; full description restored in output.
